text: The surgical team positions the patient in a dead lateral orientation, with the port placed near the umbilicus for a supravenous approach.

The port is positioned in the midclavicular line under the costal margin, approximately two fingers below. Upon entering the area, the spleen is visible, including its medial aspect, within the abdominal cavity.
A 5mm port is used to introduce a gas stream into the surgical field, facilitating visualization and access during the adrenalectomy procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, lowering it to access the space between the splenic flexure and the kidney. The dissection continues between the kidney, pancreas, and spleen, navigating the pancreatic-spanning clock plane.
The surgeon demonstrates the anatomical plane between the colon and the lower part of the kidney, highlighting the spatial relationship between these structures.
The surgeon identifies the entry point in the surgical plane, lifting the spleen and pancreas to access the pancreatic lock area.
The surgeon exposes the suprarenal gland, which begins to appear with a golden yellow hue. The anatomical plane is visible, providing a clear view of the surgical field.

The surgeon positions the patient laterally to utilize gravity for mobilizing the spinopancreatic block, revealing the pancreas. The pancreas is visible, and the surgeon carefully manipulates it to approach the spleen, distal pancreas, suprarenal gland, and kidney, emphasizing the importance of maintaining clear planes between these organs.

The surgeon continues to develop the plane between the pancreas, suprarenal gland, and kidney, further opening the space to mobilize the splenopancreatic block. The block is moved away from the suprarenal gland to expose the inferior phrenic vessels, which are the important landmarks.

The surgeon continues to expand the surgical space, revealing the phrenic vessels and the left adrenal vein. The phrenic vessels are visible, and the adrenal vein can be seen in the fibrous part of the image. The splenopancreatic block has been removed, allowing clear visualization of the vessels emerging from the phrenic side.

The surgeon enters the space below the suprarenal gland and above the diaphragm, lifting the suprarenal gland from the diaphragm. The diaphragm muscle is clearly visible, and small branches from the left side of the phrenic vessels are observed entering the suprarenal gland. The space is fully opened, allowing access along the phrenic vessels without detaching the suprarenal gland from the diaphragmatic surface.

The surgeon accesses the vein below by developing the space above, detaching the attachment between the suprarenal gland and the spleen. The spinopancreatic block is visible, extending from the gland to the head.

The surgeon uses a 5mm Megashock device to control the adrenal vessels emerging from the left side of the phrenic cradle. The adrenal pedicle is exposed by expanding the vein, revealing the renal capsule plane above the kidney. The renal vasectomy is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the large adrenal vein, which runs perpendicular to the artery from the suprarenal side. The adrenal vein is ligated, sealed, and cut, allowing for further dissection in a lateral direction while maintaining visual awareness of the renal vessels below.

The surgeon carefully removes the fat surrounding the suprarenal gland, ensuring that the adrenal gland itself remains intact. The dissection is performed at a higher level than the renal disc, staying outside the adrenal capsule.

The surgeon identifies the upper pole of the kidney and begins cutting the perinephric fat at this level to expose the kidney capsule. Once the capsule is visible, the fat is removed in the suprarenal direction. Pneumo peritoneum is inserted to facilitate dissection, and blunt dissection is used to separate the fat from the kidney capsule without damaging the kidney. The renal vasculature near the upper pole is carefully managed as the dissection progresses laterally, avoiding the hilum.

The surgeon visualizes the upper polar renal vessel and secures it at the apex of the upper pole. The pre-nephric path is intact, ensuring the adrenal gland is not violated. The plane is maintained correctly for the complete excision of the adrenal area between the perineal gland and the upper pole.

The surgeon continues the dissection through the perinephric and retroperitoneal fat surrounding the suprarenal gland, carefully preserving the integrity of the adrenal capsule to ensure the mass is removed intact without any capsule violation.
The diaphragm is clearly visible and controlled throughout the operation, with no manipulation or grasping of the adrenal gland observed.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the adrenal gland by gently pushing and lifting it without grasping, to avoid rupturing the capsule. The surrounding crinophilic fat is used for leverage during this maneuver.

The adrenal gland is visually free from surrounding attachments and is placed into an endobag for protection. The bed of the adrenal gland is visible, and the gland is carefully maneuvered without grasping to ensure it is securely placed in the endobag for externalization.
